sentences.text: We were trying to figure out the partisans and the-- and the situation that drew you in.
Lesno Brdo was a beautiful setting.
We had a little house, a small castle, I would say, on the tip of a little mountain.
And we looked down into a valley.
And we were surrounded by high mountains, all with beautiful trees and very, very small farms.
And self-sustaining farms, which means the farmers really didn't need anything.
Perhaps they need shoes and clothes, but the rest, foodstuffs, they had themselves.
And that was very, very important at that time.
Because we had nothing to eat.
And in an legitimate way, buying food was only possible with very restrictive cards.
So we developed something.
We sent out teenagers, me and other boys and girls, to the farmers.
And we had to go farther and father and farther.
And we bought potatoes, because, don't forget, money we had more than food.
We bought potatoes.
And we bought, for example, eggs.
And we bought [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] carrots.
And we bought farm products.
And we brought them back to the-- to-- to our place.
And in order to get more money, we developed an a little line to-- to Ljubljana.
And we sold.
We actually sold surplus eggs and butter and potatoes to the people in-- in-- in-- in Ljubljana, which were also short on food.
So we had a barter going on.
We got shoes and we got clothes for a few eggs and so on.
And that worked out pretty well.
We were still hungry.
We were still not over fed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
But the political situation changed.
Suddenly, we heard about shootings.
Some-- suddenly, during the night, we were standing on the balcony, and we say-- we saw on-- on one mountain, fires, and then on the second one.
And the next day, we heard this farmhouse was burned by the-- by the Italian troops, because they had supported the partisans.
And this house was burned by the partisans, because this farmer sold food to the Italians.
And we were more or less in the middle of-- and the fighting starting to-- getting worse by day.
And finally, one day, the partisans came.
They came to us.
And Joschko, of course is fluent in-- in the Yugoslavian language.
And he-- he told them about the-- about our situation.
And they were very supportive about us, more so when they heard that we were not friendly with the Germans at all.
Because they thought German-- they didn't understand Jews.
For them, Jews was-- what is a Jew?
They had no idea.
But they knew we were German or we were Austrian.
So finally, they realized that we-- we were persecuted.
And then they brought us food.
And then a friendlier relationship started to begin with the partisans, with Cernij, their leader.
Who was it?
Cernij, that was his name.
It is not easy for me.
It is S-Z-R-N-Y.
I think there's a thing like this on top of the S or something, Cernij.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the meantime, the Italian troops, in the same area, found out that the partisans are in the area too.
And they started to make sweeps.
And they came also to us.
And they said, are the partisans in this area?
And we said, of course, no.
And, a matter of fact, one day, we had a wounded partisan hiding upstairs.
But the situation went from good to bad.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
I must also tell you that some of the farmers had radios.
And those radios were able to listen to the BBC.
So at that time, we had already knowledge from BBC.
And I remember the victory-- you know, the signals and so on.
And we knew how the political situation or the-- or the war was going on.
I was perhaps 18, now.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] finally, we got the notification, from the Italian provincial government, that they intend us to be moved to Italy, proper, in internment.
Now this is not freedom, like we have in Grad Lesno Brdo, but this is interment-- internment in a little village, by the name of Nonantola, in the vicinity of Modena.
But before we could leave Lesno Brdo, we had also to get permission from the partisans to cross their line.
And the partisans, at that time, they wanted the older children, boys and girls, to join them.
And a matter of fact, one of our girls joined them, by the name of Lili Neuman.
And she was, later on, killed.
She and another one were the only person we lost during the whole time for the war.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When Joschko made Cernij see our situation, Cernij took the barricades away.

What I forgot to tell you is that the partisans, when the fighting with the Italians started, they cut down all the trees on the road going to Horjul, to the main-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] to the main area.
We were an isolated building.
And they-- actually, the-- the houses were in an-- in an little area called Horjul.
But the road, the-- the partisans closed by cutting down all the trees.
And the trees were laying over the road.
That way, they prevented their-- the Italian cars coming up certain roads.
They made barriers.
We walked.
And we had one of their horse-drawn carriages for our, you know belongings.
And we went to Ljubljana.
And we were put on one railroad car.
And we were hooked up to the train.
And the train went from Ljubljana, to Postumia Grotte, to Trieste, and from Trieste up Bologna to Modena.
And an interesting thing is that the Germans knew about our group, too.

Because they requested the Italian government, the Italian local government, when they requested the-- the-- the-- the wagon, the railway car, for us, to send the car, instead of Trieste, Bologna, Modena, to Trevizo and up to Germany.
And their-- their request was actually denied by the Italian government.
Right.

And when we arrived in Modena, after I think a day or day and a half long journey, the Modena-- the Modena Jewish community-- they have a beautiful temple there-- took us in-- in-- into their temple and provided food and shelter for the children, who were exhausted at that time.
I forgot to tell you.
Joschko and our group of 10 adults, I should say-- Joschko was the main-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and he, perhaps, was the brain of the group.
You know, he was the-- the-- the direct-- giving the directions.
But we always had contact with the outside world.
And this is an amazing fact.
We were in contact in-- when we were in Lesno Brdo, in '41 and '42, with Switzerland, with the the only shaliach in whole Europe, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in-- in Switzerland.
Shaliach is an-- a Jew sent from Palestine, from one Jewish community, the-- from one of the kibbutzim, to Europe, on sometimes not very legal ways.
And he has the the order to help other Jews.
And I would say he has, also, the unlimited financial power.
By unlimited, I mean he had the ways of getting money from the United States, from South America, from-- from Palestine, from Turkey, from any other ways.
He-- he had a-- the facility.
And he had, also, the order to give out this money to anybody in Europe requesting help.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] If you give me one second, I have it.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
We made contact with-- with-- with him.
And we-- we were in written contact, sometimes not direct.
Sometimes devious notes were-- were sent.
But they knew of our existence.
And they diverted money, from Switzerland to Yugoslavia.
And they diverted money, also, from Switzerland to Italy, when we were in Italy.
And they supported us, very-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very well.
Hm?
Hm?
The Jewish community, under DELASEM-- the Jewish community, under the name of DELASEM--
DELASEM is the same thing, like the Israeli Kultusgemeinde in Zagreb, was the-- was the-- Israelitische 'hilfs' organization in Italy, located in Geneva-- in Genoa.
And the-- the man in Switzerland, in Geneva, his name is Nathan Schwalb.
And he was a member of a kibbutz in-- in Israel.
I would have to--
S-C-H-W-A-L-B, Nathan.
He was the shaliach, yeah.
And we had another man, by the name of Lichtheim, Dr.
Lichtheim in-- in Switzerland.
And by the way, I-- I met both of them later on.
And Nathan Schwalb came back to Palestine after the war.
He was awarded all kind of recognition for his saving and doing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In Nonantola, the DELASEM rented an villa, which belonged to a very rich Jew, by the name of Villa Emma.
This building still exists today.
At that time, it was empty.
It was a building, perhaps, with 20 rooms, with a large kitchen, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with running water, with electricity-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the ideal thing for a home for children.
They rented it for us.
And it was sparsely furnished.
His name, I don't know.
He was an-- a Modenese Jew.
And we really never met him.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we actually continued our life in Modena, in villa Nonantola, the same way like we had in Lesno Brdo.
Our school continued.
We were very, very fortunate.
We had never any sicknesses, because that would have been disastrous.
And we had also developed a system where the clothes was handed down, from one to the next, so if somebody grew out, somebody else had it.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Nonantola was more freedom for us.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The DELASEM sent an director to Nonantola, by the name of Jaccia, a Dr.
Jaccia, and also an-- an woman doctor.
And they were actually in charge of having contact with the Italian authorities.
And Joschko was in charge of the children.
But they were in charge of the contact with the Italian authorities.
We started to learn Italian.
There were classes.
And in the end, I spoke, quite fluently, Italian.
But in the beginning, Joschko couldn't speak Italian, none of the people in the group.
So the Jewish community sent Jaccia, Dr.
Jaccia, which is an Italian Jew, to us as a--
J-A-C-C-I-A, I believe, Jaccia.
And by the way, when we escaped to Switzerland, he didn't come with us.
He stayed in Italy.
And the-- and all the other people stayed in Italy.
They didn't go with us.
And our school existed.
And a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a few months later, an second group of children, this time Yugoslavian children, reached us.
Yes, Yugoslavia was invaded from Germany.
And the situation's gotten worse and worse.
And finally, an group of children was sent from Yugoslavia, from the city of Split, to Nonantola, to join us.
And this group was 38--
--38 children.
The interesting part is-- I remember, when they came, there was one child, barely four years old.
And they picked up on the street of Sarajevo, crying, alone.
And he couldn't remember his name.
So Joschko and the other adults, not knowing what to do, they actually gave him a name, Aron Kahn.
They didn't want it to say, 'Cohen,' so they said, Kahn.
Cohen is, you know, the Jews, Cohen-Levi, so.
And he joined our group.
So now the age changed.
Now we were from 4 to 17, or from 4 to 18, perhaps, with the help of around 12, 13 adults.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] an woodworker, a Jewish woodworker joined us, sent from the ORT.
ORT found him in one of the internment camps in Italy.
And he came to us.
And he started an woodworking school in Nonantola.
So that was the first ORT group, during the war, working in occupied-- in-- in-- in Italy.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we also started to work by farmers, in order to learn the agricultural work.
And we also sent children in the-- teenagers in the factory of Nonantola.
There was an-- a factory making juice.
I think it was shipped to Germany, not for our consumption.
And how-- how long did this last in Villa Emma?
Villa Emma lasted [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] quite a long time.
It lasted till the British and American forces started to land in Anzio.
And it changed in the moment when Mussolini was-- had to abdicate, and Marshal Badoglio took over the government of the-- of Italy.
The King Emmanuel was just a figurehead.
And Badoglio tried to make peace or armistice, with the British and American forces.
But the Germans were against it.
And they started to occupy Italy.
They liberated Mussolini from Gran Sasso.
They came down the boot.
They came down from Austria.
They actually occupied Italy.
A lot of Italian soldiers tried to escape, tried to go underground.
And our camp, our Villa Emma, at that time, was requested as a headquarters or as an building for the German occupying forces.
And we had to leave.

But before I come to that, I would like to tell you that Joschko established contact, with the cloister and the church in Nonantola, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and with one of the doctors, which came occasionally to our children when they had colds or not really serious sicknesses.
But he came, Dr.
Bocari.
And when we had to leave Nonantola, it was rather sudden.
When we had to leave Villa Emma, it was rather sudden.
Yes, the German, as well as the fascists, knew.
Because-- I don't know if I told you before-- the Italian government, the fascistic Italian government, partially supported our group, financially.
Because it was an internment camp.
The Villa Emma was an-- a camp.
It was a villa.
But we had no-- we-- we had-- were restricted in movement.
We had to-- we were understood strict carabinieri, which is the police, control.
And they supported, actually, financially, partially, while the rest was supported by the-- by the Italian Jewish community, by the DELASEM.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So when we had suddenly to-- to leave Villa Emma, before we had to do that, Joschko went to the priests and Dr.
Bocari and asked them if they-- if-- the existing cloister in Nonantola is a big cloister, for nuns and for novices but only for girls-- if they would take in the children.
And after a little bartering, they say, yes.
We take all children, girls, and my mother, a-- a woman.
And we take you if you take the priest-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] abbot.
And you can stay with us.
But we don't take the boys, the-- the teenage boys.
They took only the boys up to, I believe, 14-- or perhaps-- up till 14.
But the bigger boys, they couldn't take.
Because they had only an woman close-there-- cloister.
And they had a lot of girl novices and so on.
They didn't-- they couldn't.
But the farmers in Nonantola and surrounding, they were told, by the priest, take the boys.
And we took shelter.
And we got shelter, with the farmers of Nonantola.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] His name is in the Yad Vashem in Palestine.
And they planted trees in his name.
And he got recognition as one of the 'Righteous Ones.' And if you give me a few moments, I will remember.
But right now, I don't remember.
But he and Dr.
Bocari were brought to Israel after the war in, I think, '85.
And they were bestowed the honor of the 'Righteous People.' And trees were planted in-- in his name.
And all of children, which were in Palestine, at that time, came to this occasion.
And there was a big celebration.
So he was recognized for that.
OK.
That name, I should know.
Oh, God.
